Q1: I usually organized my study routine around hands-on practice rather than just reading. I'd set aside about 18 hours a week, spreading it across most days, and I'd try to actually do things with the material—work through problems, apply concepts, that kind of thing. I found that sitting still and just watching videos wasn't really my style, so I'd often pause and try things out myself. My routine was fairly consistent, though I'll admit my motivation wasn't always where I wanted it to be.
Q2: My pattern was mostly regular, but it definitely shifted when deadlines were approaching. I'd try to keep a steady rhythm during normal weeks, maybe studying a few hours each day. But when assignments were due, I'd ramp things up and spend longer sessions working through the material. Sometimes other life stuff got in the way too, so I'd have to adjust, but overall I kept things relatively predictable.
Q3: Honestly, I'd log into the LMS mainly when I knew there was something I needed to check—like if an assignment was posted or if there were updates to the course. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if there was anything new, but usually there was a specific trigger, like a notification or knowing a deadline was coming. It wasn't always excitement that got me there; sometimes it was more like obligation.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks it did become part of my routine. I'd usually check the LMS in the morning with my coffee, just to see what was happening. The peer support group I was part of helped with that—we'd remind each other to check in and discuss things, so it became a social habit as much as an academic one. It wasn't forced after a while; it just happened naturally.
Q5: The video lectures were actually pretty useful, especially when I could pause and try things myself. I watched a lot of video content—over 160 minutes worth—because I could control the pace. The discussion posts were helpful too when other students explained concepts in their own words. I'm a kinesthetic learner, so anything that let me engage actively felt more valuable than just reading text.
Q6: Some of the reading materials I'd skip or skim through quickly. If it was just dense text without any interactive element, I'd lose focus pretty fast. I also didn't complete all the optional tasks—my task completion was only around 45%—because I prioritized what I knew would count for my grade. It wasn't that those resources were bad; I just had to choose where to put my energy.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it connected to the assignments or exams. If I could tell it would help me actually perform better, I'd invest in it. I also valued things that let me do something rather than just absorb information passively. When I could feel myself learning through practice, that's when I knew my time was well spent.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different. There was less pressure with them, which was nice, but that also meant I'd push them aside when stress built up. Required activities had this urgency that made me prioritize them no matter what. With optional stuff, I'd think 'I'll get to that later,' and sometimes later never came. It's a trade-off between freedom and motivation.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download materials and work through them on my own, sometimes printing things out so I could annotate and move around while studying. I'd also use external resources like YouTube tutorials or textbooks I had from previous courses. The LMS was just one piece of my study toolkit, not the whole thing.
Q10: There were many times I'd be working through problems on paper or practicing concepts physically—like mapping out processes or building models—and none of that showed up in the LMS. I'd spend hours doing hands-on practice that was invisible digitally. My exam scores reflect that work, but if you only looked at my online activity, you'd miss most of it.
Q11: Yeah, I did this regularly. I'd download a lecture video or PDF once, then study from it multiple times offline. I didn't see the point of streaming the same video repeatedly when I could just have it saved. This meant my LMS engagement looked lower than my actual engagement with the material. It was more efficient for me to work that way.
Q12: There were definitely moments when things got harder. When my stress levels peaked—and they did get pretty high—I found it tough to muster the motivation to engage. Sometimes I'd feel this moderate anxiety about keeping up, and combined with my naturally low motivation, it made logging in feel like a chore. My mood would dip and everything felt heavier.
Q13: Workload and competing responsibilities definitely affected how I used the LMS. When I had multiple deadlines converging, I'd become very selective about what I engaged with. Fatigue played a role too—after long study sessions, I'd just want to step away from screens entirely. I'd sometimes feel confused by certain topics and that made me avoid those sections temporarily.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd be logged in, scrolling through materials, but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd watch a video but not really absorb it because I was stressed or tired. Those sessions felt unproductive even though they'd show up as engagement in the logs. Being present isn't the same as being mentally engaged.
Q15: I definitely postponed things, especially when my motivation was low. Even knowing something was important, I'd sometimes avoid it because the mental energy required felt too much. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better. This was especially true for tasks that seemed tedious or didn't involve hands-on work. It's something I struggled with throughout the course.
Q16: Deadlines were honestly my main driver. I'd ramp up my LMS usage significantly as deadlines approached. Without that external pressure, I'd sometimes coast. The structure of having due dates helped me manage my naturally low motivation—it gave me a concrete reason to push through even when I didn't feel like it. My best work happened under that time pressure.
Q17: Feedback did affect my engagement, though I wished there was more of it. When I got positive feedback on my assignments—and my scores were really high—it gave me a boost. But my overall mood stayed pretty low despite doing well, so the impact was temporary. I'd engage more right after getting feedback, then gradually slip back into my usual patterns.
Q18: The course structure helped me stay on track because it was clear what was expected and when. Having those regular deadlines and organized modules made it easier to plan my 18 hours of study each week. But sometimes the structure felt rigid, and I wished there was more flexibility for different learning styles. The peer support intervention helped me feel more connected to the course.
Q19: The video content was designed well for someone like me who learns by doing—I could pause and practice along. But some of the discussion requirements felt forced; I only posted twice because I didn't always have something meaningful to add. The course could have used more interactive simulations or hands-on activities. Those would have engaged me more naturally.
Q20: I think that happens when students are trying to figure things out but struggling to produce finished work. They might be exploring, getting confused, starting and stopping. Or maybe they're dealing with stress and anxiety that makes it hard to complete things even though they're trying. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity—sometimes it's just spinning wheels.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me. My LMS activity was moderate, but my exam scores and assignment quality were high. I did a lot of learning offline—downloading materials, practicing on my own, studying from notes. The LMS only captured part of my engagement. You can be deeply engaged with course content without it showing up in digital logs.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in, how long you watch videos, and what you click on. That's useful for seeing general trends. But they miss the quality of engagement—whether you're actually thinking deeply or just going through motions. They also miss offline study, emotional states, and the real understanding that happens away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm actively thinking, questioning, and connecting ideas to things I already know. For me, it's also physical—moving around, writing things out, practicing hands-on. True engagement is when I lose track of time because I'm genuinely absorbed in learning.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with motivation even while performing well. It wouldn't capture the stress I felt or how my mood stayed low despite my good grades. It also wouldn't show all the offline studying I did, the peer conversations that helped me, or the mental effort behind my high scores. The numbers tell one story, but there's a lot more underneath.
